3555 Las Vegas Blvd S.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
On the morning of March 3, 2026, an incident occurred when a member of the public was able to gain access to the
flamingo island housing six flamingos. According to facility records, the actions by this individual during the incident
resulted in serious animal harm with painful injuries to three of the flamingos (Peachy, Blackjack and Bugsy). Blackjack
and Bugsy were grabbed and held down in the habitat by the individual. Peachy was captured by the individual, removed
from the habitat, and taken to a hotel room. The following injuries noted directly from the veterinary records on March 5,
2026, include:
-Peachy: 27 year old male, approximately 7-8 black flight feathers missing from left wing - 2 remaining flight feathers; 2
black flight feathers missing from right wing - 9-10 feathers remaining; broken feather shafts and blood noted on left wing
feathers; missing feathers from right carpus, sensitive upon palpation of left wing. Assessment: Broken/Missing feathers
DDx: trauma due to plucking vs blood feathers vs open.
-Blackjack: 41 year old female, down feathers missing on dorsal and ventral aspect of right wing; inflammed and erythema
to pinion site of right wing; sensitive upon extension of both wings - more significant on left wing. Inflammation to her
pinion site and feather loss most consistent with trauma.
-Bugsy: 41 year old male. Assessment: Cloacal bulging-DDx: suspect mild prolapsing due to trauma vs parasites vs open.
According to the wildlife curator, the above injuries/conditions were not present or noticed prior to the incident. And
immediately after the incident some of Blackjack’s feathers were found on the ground in the habitat and several of
Peachy’s feathers were found in the hotel room.
Following the incident, the facility promptly communicated with the Attending Veterinarian(AV) by phone and video
concerning the flamingos’ health status. The facility received instructions which the AV confirmed were followed on March
3rd and 4th. Discussions with the facility and the AV confirmed there were no in-person exams or evaluations performed
by a veterinarian on the affected flamingos until two days later, March 5th. It was confirmed Peachy was given pain
medication by an animal care team member later in the day on March 3rd as directed by the AV. However, flamingos,
Blackjack and Bugsy did not receive pain medications until March 5th. Due to the nature of the incident with traumatic and
painful injuries, delaying an in-person examination and treatment for 2 days by a veterinarian was not a timely or
appropriate method to diagnose and treat injuries. Blackjack and Bugsy experienced unrelieved pain for two days
following the incident. Additionally, Peachy experienced unrelieved pain for several hours until he received his pain
medication later that day.
Each exhibitor must establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include the use of appropriate
methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries, and the availability of emergency, weekend and
holiday care.
Correct from this day, 03/20/2026, forward.

On March 3, 2026, an incident occurred and the facility handled its six flamingos in a manner which failed to assure
animal safety. This resulted in harm to the animals due to insufficient barriers between the animals and the public. In the
early morning hours, a member of the public was able to gain access to the flamingo island and subsequently chase and
harass the flamingo flock and eventually physically harm three of them. According to facility records and based on their
video surveillance review, the individual cornered and held down two flamingos (Bugsy and Blackjack); however, they
were able to free themselves. Then, the individual physically captured flamingo, Peachy, and exited the island carrying
the animal through the casino to the elevator and then up to a hotel room. Peachy was recovered and secured by the
facility that morning. Discussions with the wildlife curator and review of veterinary records indicate that flamingo, Peachy,
suffered traumatic injuries to both wings including several of his flight feathers being removed/plucked. Flamingo,
Blackjack’s injuries included inflammation to her pinion site and feather loss most consistent with trauma. Flamingo,
Bugsy’s injury included a mild prolapsing of the cloaca suspected due to stress and/or trauma.
Ineffective barriers which allow the public to gain access to animal housing areas does not ensure the safety of the public
or the animals. Members of the public may become injured if the animals become unexpectedly startled or stressed, and
the animals may experience severe behavioral stress, injury or death from encountering the public inside their enclosure.
During public exhibition, including after hours, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal
and to the public, with sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to
assure the safely of the animals and the public.
Correct from this day, 03/20/2026, forward.

The following areas of the outdoor facility housing several free-roaming duck species were in disrepair.
-mermaid pond: There was an approximately 2ft.X1ft. opening/hole along the top surface of a large rock feature adjacent
to the pond.
-first island: There was a large surface crack along one of the sides of the rocks approximately 1in. wide and 1.5ft long.
-stairs to chapel: There was an approximately 1.5ft.X 1.5ft opening/hole at the base of a large rock near the stairs.
-back garden area: There was an approximately 6ft. section of plastic fencing separating the pond from the back garden
area lying loosely folded on the ground.
Birds may potentially become trapped or injured in these openings/holes and cracks in the rocks or in the loose fencing
material within their enclosure. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained in good repair so that they protect
the birds from injury.

There were accumulations of various coins along the bottom of the ponds in front of both the Pelican island and Flamingo
island. Also, there was a pair of discarded sunglasses observed on the bottom of the Pelican island pond. At the back
side of the Flamingo island, there was a heavy accumulation of clam and mussel shells from food waste. The 44 free-
roaming ducks at the facility have access to these pond areas.
Foreign objects and coins thrown into the ponds pose a risk of ingestion hazard and potential toxicity to the ducks.
Accumulations of food waste may also affect water quality and pose a health risk to the animals with access.
Particulate animal and food waste, trash, debris that enters the pool or other aquatic area must be removed as often as
necessary to maintain the required water quality and minimize health hazards to the birds.
Correct by 04/03/2026.

There was not a sufficient number of employees or attendants present at time of inspection to maintain a professionally
acceptable level of husbandry. There were accumulations of food waste, such as clams and mussel shells along the
bottom of the pond behind the flamingo island. There were accumulations of coins on the bottoms of the pools in front of
the flamingo island and pelican island as well as a pair of discarded sunglasses. All 44 free-roaming ducks at the facility
have access to these pond areas. At time of inspection there was only one animal caretaker responsible for animal care,
husbandry and daily observations. Additionally, according to the facility representative there are consistent occurrences of
only one animal caretaker available on some days. An insufficient number of employees available to observe and care for
the animals and animal areas on a daily basis may negatively affect the health and well-being of the animals. A sufficient
number of adequately trained employees and attendants must be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level
of husbandry set forth in this subpart.
Correct by 04/03/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
ALEXANDRA ANDRICOS, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Critical
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
On the morning of March 3, 2026, an incident occurred when a member of the public was able to gain access to the

According to the wildlife curator, the above injuries/conditions were not present or noticed prior to the incident. And

2.131(c)(1)  Critical
   
Handling of animals. 
On March 3, 2026, an incident occurred and the facility handled its six flamingos in a manner which failed to assure 
animal safety. This resulted in harm to the animals due to insufficient barriers between the animals and the public. In the 
early morning hours, a member of the public was able to gain access to the flamingo island and subsequently chase and 
harass the flamingo flock and eventually physically harm three of them. According to facility records and based on their 
video surveillance review, the individual cornered and held down two flamingos (Bugsy and Blackjack); however, they 
were able to free themselves. Then, the individual physically captured flamingo, Peachy, and exited the island carrying 
the animal through the casino to the elevator and then up to a hotel room. Peachy was recovered and secured by the 
facility that morning. Discussions with the wildlife curator and review of veterinary records indicate that flamingo, Peachy, 
suffered traumatic injuries to both wings including several of his flight feathers being removed/plucked. Flamingo, 
Blackjack’s injuries included inflammation to her pinion site and feather loss most consistent with trauma. Flamingo, 
Bugsy’s injury included a mild prolapsing of the cloaca suspected due to stress and/or trauma. 
 
Ineffective barriers which allow the public to gain access to animal housing areas does not ensure the safety of the public 
or the animals. Members of the public may become injured if the animals become unexpectedly startled or stressed, and 
the animals may experience severe behavioral stress, injury or death from encountering the public inside their enclosure. 
During public exhibition, including after hours, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal 
and to the public, with sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to 
assure the safely of the animals and the public. 
 
Correct from this day, 03/20/2026, forward. 
3.153(a)(1)(ii)  Repeat
   
Primary enclosures. 
The following areas of the outdoor facility housing several free-roaming duck species were in disrepair. 
 
-mermaid pond: There was an approximately 2ft.X1ft. opening/hole along the top surface of a large rock feature adjacent 
to the pond. 
 
-first island: There was a large surface crack along one of the sides of the rocks approximately 1in. wide and 1.5ft long. 
 
-stairs to chapel: There was an approximately 1.5ft.X 1.5ft opening/hole at the base of a large rock near the stairs.

-back garden area: There was an approximately 6ft. section of plastic fencing separating the pond from the back garden 
area lying loosely folded on the ground. 
 
Birds may potentially become trapped or injured in these openings/holes and cracks in the rocks or in the loose fencing 
material within their enclosure. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained in good repair so that they protect 
the birds from injury. 
3.157(a)(1) 
   
Water quality. 
There were accumulations of various coins along the bottom of the ponds in front of both the Pelican island and Flamingo 
island. Also, there was a pair of discarded sunglasses observed on the bottom of the Pelican island pond. At the back 
side of the Flamingo island, there was a heavy accumulation of clam and mussel shells from food waste. The 44 free-
roaming ducks at the facility have access to these pond areas.  
Foreign objects and coins thrown into the ponds pose a risk of ingestion hazard and potential toxicity to the ducks. 
Accumulations of food waste may also affect water quality and pose a health risk to the animals with access.  
Particulate animal and food waste, trash, debris that enters the pool or other aquatic area must be removed as often as 
necessary to maintain the required water quality and minimize health hazards to the birds. 
 
Correct by 04/03/2026. 
3.159 
   
Employees 
There was not a sufficient number of employees or attendants present at time of inspection to maintain a professionally 
acceptable level of husbandry. There were accumulations of food waste, such as clams and mussel shells along the 
bottom of the pond behind the flamingo island. There were accumulations of coins on the bottoms of the pools in front of 
the flamingo island and pelican island as well as a pair of discarded sunglasses. All 44 free-roaming ducks at the facility 
have access to these pond areas. At time of inspection there was only one animal caretaker responsible for animal care, 
husbandry and daily observations. Additionally, according to the facility representative there are consistent occurrences of 
only one animal caretaker available on some days. An insufficient number of employees available to observe and care for 
the animals and animal areas on a daily basis may negatively affect the health and well-being of the animals. A sufficient 
number of adequately trained employees and attendants must be utilized to maintain the professionally acceptable level 
of husbandry set forth in this subpart.  
 
Correct by 04/03/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.

Additional Inspectors: 
ALEXANDRA ANDRICOS, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
